message:
refactor(storage): drop fsqlite-planner workarounds now that upstream is fixed

Upstream frankensqlite has landed the planner and prepared-statement
fixes that beads_rust was working around, and the new
`crates/fsqlite-core/tests/beads_rebuild_fresh_lookup.rs` regression
test on the fsqlite side proves the reference behaviour directly on
`Connection`. The storage layer here can now go back to the obvious
single-row keyed reads without trading correctness for the workaround
machinery.

Removed workarounds
-------------------

- `scan_issue_from_conn` — the full-scan fallback added under #256 for
  the "row visible to `br list` but invisible to the direct lookup"
  failure mode.
- `recover_issue_lookup_via_scan` — its wrapper that logged a warn
  when the scan recovered a row that the primary keyed lookup missed.
  Both are gone; `get_issue_from_conn` now does a single
  `query_row_with_params("SELECT … FROM issues WHERE id = ?")` and
  treats `FrankenError::QueryReturnedNoRows` as a clean `Ok(None)`.
- CTE wrapper in `get_issue_from_conn` (the
  `WITH target(id_value) AS (SELECT ?)` form added under #252/#226 to
  force `prepared_select_requires_dispatch` into the slow path). The
  lookup now returns the plain `SELECT … WHERE id = ?` and relies on
  the upstream planner fix.
- CTE wrapper in `create_issue`'s duplicate-ID pre-check (#254): the
  check runs via `query_row_with_params` and matches on
  `QueryReturnedNoRows` for "not present".
- CTE wrapper in the `update_issue` claim guard (#254) that read back
  the current assignee for the "issue already assigned" validation
  message; same shape — `query_row_with_params` plus a
  `QueryReturnedNoRows` branch returning `"<unknown>"`.
- Single-row `query_with_params` + `.into_iter().next()` fallbacks in
  `get_parent_id`, `get_config`, and `get_export_hash` (added under
  #229). All three now use `query_row_with_params` directly.

`get_issue_from_conn` keeps the defensive ID-mismatch check from #255
— if the planner ever hands back a row whose `id` doesn't equal the
requested one, we still return `BeadsError::Other("storage
consistency: …")` rather than a silently wrong row. That's a cheap
invariant to leave in and the one piece of defensive code that isn't
paying for a known upstream bug.

sync.rs companion change
------------------------

The pre-import `--rebuild` shortcut introduced earlier in this branch
grows one more condition: `open_result.paths.jsonl_path.is_file()`.
`recover_database_from_jsonl` runs a preflight that fails hard on a
missing JSONL, but `execute_import` already degrades gracefully when
the JSONL is absent. Gating the shortcut on `jsonl_path.is_file()`
keeps that graceful-missing-JSONL path, and avoids the confusing
hard failure users saw when running `br sync --rebuild` in a beads
dir with a corrupt-but-recoverable DB and no JSONL yet.

Tests
-----

- `tests/e2e_issue_252_fresh_bead_lookup.rs` — the doc comment is
  rewritten to describe the invariant in behavioural terms ("fresh
  IDs must be visible to every ID-lookup entry point") rather than
  referencing the old CTE-dispatch mechanism. The actual test body is
  unchanged: create N beads back-to-back, resolve each via `show`,
  `update`, and `defer`. It is kept as a guard against the original
  failure mode returning through any future query-strategy change.
- `tests/e2e_raw_fsqlite_rebuilt_lookup.rs` — grows a second flow
  that exercises the `beads_rust::storage::SqliteStorage` path end
  to end after the rebuild, not just the raw fsqlite `Connection`.
  That is where the removed workarounds lived, so the test now
  proves the direct keyed path (`SqliteStorage::get_issue`,
  `get_parent_id`, `get_config`, `get_export_hash`) returns the same
  answers as a full scan across a rebuilt DB.

Net effect: ~80 fewer lines of workaround code, one coherent keyed
read path across `SqliteStorage`, and the invariants that were
implicit in the CTE-wrapped queries are now pinned by the new
fsqlite-side regression test plus the expanded end-to-end harness
here.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
